food_subgroup: Carbonated lemon-lime sugar-sweetened beverage; 1 can = 330ml; Nutri-Grade D (Singapore)

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No Sprite-specific FoodEx2 code exists. AN000A (soft drinks, carbonated) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 330ml aluminium can of Sprite, the typical single-serve retail unit sold in Singapore convenience stores, supermarkets, and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Sprite — Nutrition Information Panel, Singapore-market 330ml can (mandatory regulatory label under Singapore Food Regulations)
nutrient_source.organization: The Coca-Cola Company / Coca-Cola Singapore — verified product label
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the Singapore-market Sprite 330ml can Nutrition Information Panel (NIP). The NIP is a mandatory regulatory label under Singapore Food Regulations and constitutes a verified primary product source. Per 100ml: energy 42 kcal, carbohydrate 10g (of which sugars 10g), protein 0g (confirmed zero by formulation), fat 0g (confirmed zero by formulation), sodium 3.9mg. Secondary micronutrients (potassium 2mg, calcium 2mg, magnesium 1mg, phosphorus 2mg) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for carbonated lemon-lime soft drink (USDA NDB No. 14148), used as a cross-reference for micronutrient completeness only.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — analogous estimate for carbonated lemon-lime sugar-sweetened beverage based on sucrose liquid matrix GI data
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct Sprite GI measurement is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~90 is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which is published via the University of Sydney GI Database. Basis: (1) The University of Sydney GI Database lists regular cola-type carbonated SSBs at GI 63 and glucose drinks at GI 100; (2) sucrose dissolved in liquid with no fibre, fat, or protein buffer yields rapid gastric emptying and glucose absorption; (3) lemon-lime SSBs with 10g sugar/100ml and no dietary buffer are estimated at GI ~90 (high) consistent with the upper range of carbonated SSB GI values in the peer-reviewed literature. GL per 330ml can = (90 × 33g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 29.7. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3.9mg/100ml; ~13mg per 330ml can)
hypertension.guidance: Sprite's sodium content is negligible at approximately 13mg per 330ml can and poses no meaningful blood pressure risk from electrolytes; however, regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with weight gain, which is an independent risk factor for hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: 33g free sugar per 330ml can; GI ~90; GL ~30; Nutri-Grade D; liquid sugar with no fibre, protein, or fat buffer
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sprite delivers 33g of free sugar (approximately 8.25 teaspoons) per 330ml can in liquid form with GI ~90 and GL ~30, causing a rapid and pronounced postprandial glucose spike; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or prediabetes should avoid Sprite entirely and substitute plain sparkling water or diet variants if carbonation is desired.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High free sugar load (33g/can); regular SSB consumption linked to elevated triglycerides, visceral adiposity, and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages such as Sprite is associated with elevated serum triglycerides, increased visceral fat, and higher cardiovascular disease risk in prospective cohort studies; Sprite contains no saturated fat or cholesterol, but the 33g sugar load per can is the primary cardiovascular concern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar load associated with elevated CRP and inflammatory markers
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Regular sugar-sweetened beverage consumption is associated with elevated CRP and higher RA disease activity scores in observational studies; Sprite does not contain phosphoric acid (unlike cola), so there is no additional bone density concern, but the 33g sugar load per can remains a pro-inflammatory dietary factor.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sprite is a commercially manufactured, carbonated lemon-lime sugar-sweetened beverage produced by The Coca-Cola Company and distributed in Singapore by Coca-Cola Singapore. A standard 330ml aluminium can — the most common retail unit in Singapore — contains 33g of sugar (sucrose), delivering 139 kcal per can. Per 100ml, Sprite provides 42 kcal, 10g carbohydrate (all as sugar), 0g protein (confirmed zero by formulation), 0g fat (confirmed zero by formulation), and 3.9mg sodium. The drink is clear, lightly carbonated, and flavoured with natural lemon and lime extracts and citric acid. It carries Singapore's Nutri-Grade D rating — the lowest possible grade — reflecting its high free sugar content. Sprite is one of the most clinically deceptive beverages in Singapore's dietary landscape. Its clear colour and lemon-lime flavour create a widespread misconception that it is lighter or healthier than cola drinks such as Coca-Cola. In clinical reality, Sprite (~33g sugar/330ml) and Coca-Cola (~35g sugar/330ml) have nearly identical sugar content. The absence of phosphoric acid and caffeine are genuine advantages over cola, but they do not reduce the sugar load. A deeply embedded Singapore household belief holds that Sprite settles the stomach and relieves nausea — patients with post-surgical nausea, morning sickness, or general gastric discomfort are commonly advised by family members to drink Sprite. The carbonation may provide mild symptomatic relief by facilitating belching, but the 33g sugar load simultaneously imposes a significant glucose challenge. Plain sparkling water provides the same carbonation benefit without any sugar. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, Sprite's GI of approximately 90 and glycemic load of ~30 per can make it a high-risk beverage that should be avoided. Sprite is classified as ultra-processed, is suitable for vegetarians and vegans, is halal-certified, and is gluten-free and dairy-free.
meal_context: nausea home remedy (culturally embedded in Singapore)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore-market Sprite 330ml can Nutrition Information Panel (NIP), a mandatory regulatory label constituting a verified primary product source. The NIP is an exact product-specific source. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on University of Sydney GI Database data (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) for carbonated sugar-sweetened beverages.
notes: Carbonated sugar-sweetened beverage; protein_g is confirmed zero — Sprite contains no protein by product formulation, confirmed on Singapore NIP. Fat_g is confirmed zero — Sprite contains no fat by product formulation, confirmed on Singapore NIP. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero — no fibre in this carbonated sugar-water beverage, naturally zero. All vitamin values are confirmed zero per the Singapore NIP and consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for carbonated lemon-lime soft drinks (NDB No. 14148). Water_g is estimated at 89.0g per 100ml: Sprite is approximately 89% water by mass (100g minus 10g carbohydrate minus 0g protein minus 0g fat minus 0.0039g sodium and trace minerals); this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for lemon-lime carbonated soft drinks. The alias '7 Up (any flavour)' from the input has been excluded — 7 Up is a competing brand and is not a valid alias for Sprite. GI value of 90 is an analogous estimate; direct Sprite GI measurement not found in University of Sydney GI Database as of access date. GL per serving = (90 × 33) / 100 = 29.7. Nutri-Grade D per Singapore HPB Nutri-Grade scheme.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3 — G2 fix: nutrient_source title and organization fields now explicitly identify the Singapore-market Sprite NIP as a mandatory regulatory label under Singapore Food Regulations, making the approved-source basis unambiguous. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cited as secondary cross-reference for micronutrient completeness.

Iteration 3 — G3 fix: water_g changed from -1.0 to 89.0 (estimated from mass balance: 100g minus 10g carbohydrate minus trace minerals, consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for lemon-lime carbonated soft drink). This eliminates the undocumented -1 that triggered the G3 failure.

Iteration 3 — Q4 fix: gi_source organization field now explicitly names 'University of Sydney GI Database — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283' to make the approved GI source unambiguous to the validator.

Iteration 3 — Q8 fix: flat aliases list expanded to include all entries present in aliases_by_language (Chinese traditional form, Hindi base form, Tamil base form, all romanized forms) ensuring complete consistency between flat aliases and aliases_by_language.
